definition: The Spiritual Flood — the outpouring of divine grace that Shaykh Ibrāhīm Niasse identified as the defining spiritual event of the age, predicted in the traditions of the Tijānī order. The term denotes both the historical phenomenon of Niasse's mass spiritual education movement and the metaphysical reality of divine emanation from which it draws its name.

Spiritual education and formation — the guided process by which a murshid (spiritual guide) leads a disciple toward the realization of tawḥīd. In Niasse's practice, tarbiya was a rapid and intensive method distinct from the slower methods of earlier Sufi masters.

Divine unity — the foundational Islamic doctrine of God's oneness. In Niasse's tafsīr, tawḥīd operates at multiple registers: the affirmation of God's existence (tawḥīd al-dhāt), His attributes (tawḥīd al-ṣifāt), and His acts (tawḥīd al-afʿāl). The deeper register — realised tawḥīd — is the experiential extinction of the self in the awareness of divine unity.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: فيفنى العبد ويفنى الوجود ويحصل التوحيد الحقيقي والحياة الأبدية
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The servant becomes annihilated, existence becomes annihilated, and the real tawḥīd and eternal life are attained

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: تجد فيه المواعظ والتذكير والخبر والتوحيد
niasse_evidence.translation_note: You find in it exhortation, reminder, report, and tawḥīd

definition: Annihilation — the dissolution of the ego-self in the consciousness of divine reality. Mishri defines it in relation to baqāʾ (subsistence) and locates it within the framework of the ḥaḍarāt: genuine Iman at its lowest level entails 'fanāʾ of the nafs and baqāʾ in Allah in the ḥaḍarāt of His Names and His Attributes.' In Niasse's own commentary, fanāʾ proceeds in three degrees: annihilation in the divine acts (fanāʾ fī al-afʿāl), then in the divine attributes (fanāʾ fī al-ṣifāt), then total annihilation (al-fanāʾ al-kullī) — after which 'his annihilation was identical to subsistence' (Lesson 10, compiler's apparatus, fn. 3).

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: فمن بذل ماله لله وجد الفناء في الأفعال، يعلم أن لا فاعل في الوجود إلا الله
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Whoever gives his wealth for God finds annihilation in the acts — knowing there is no agent in existence but God

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: بذكر القلب يجد الفناء في الصفات، وبذكر الروح يجد الفناء الكلي
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler on three degrees of fanāʾ: in acts (afʿāl), attributes (ṣifāt), and totality (kullī)

mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)
mishri_quote: The lowest degree of [true Iman] is fanāʾ of the nafs and baqāʾ in Allah in the ḥaḍarāt of His Names and His Attributes.

definition: Subsistence — the station that follows and completes fanāʾ (annihilation). The self does not cease to exist but subsists in and through the divine reality. As the compiler's apparatus records of Niasse's teaching: 'his annihilation was identical to subsistence' (fanāʾuhu ʿayn al-baqāʾ). The pair fanāʾ/baqāʾ constitutes the fundamental dyad of Sufi anthropology in Niasse's system, and Mishri places both within the ḥaḍarāt of the divine Names and Attributes — locating the spiritual ascent within a precise cosmological framework.
mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)
mishri_quote: The lowest degree of true Iman is fanāʾ of the nafs and baqāʾ in Allah in the ḥaḍarāt of His Names and His Attributes.

definition: Sainthood / divine friendship — the station of proximity to God attained by the awliyāʾ (saints). Niasse treats walāya as the inner dimension of prophecy, continuing after the seal of prophethood in the lineage of spiritual heirs.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: خاتما للولاية المحمدية تشريفا لهذه الأمة، فلا ولاية بعد نزول عيسى
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Sealing the Muhammadan sainthood as an honour to this community; no walāya after the descent of Jesus

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: أولياء اللّٰه بالولاية الصغرى... وأولياء اللّٰه بالولاية الكبرى
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler distinguishing walāya ṣughrā (minor sainthood) from walāya kubrā (major sainthood)

definition: Spiritual station — a stable, achieved level of spiritual realisation, as distinct from ḥāl (spiritual state), which is transient. Niasse's tafsīr maps the Quranic narrative onto a sequence of spiritual stations traversed on the path to God.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: لكل مقام مقال... مقام التذكر
niasse_evidence.translation_note: For every maqām there is a corresponding utterance... the station of recollection (tatadhakkur)

definition: Inimitability — the doctrine that the Quran cannot be matched or replicated by human composition, establishing its miraculous nature. Niasse engages this doctrine at length in his discussion of the opening suras, situating it within the broader tradition of Quranic sciences.

The disjointed letters — the letter-combinations that open twenty-nine Quranic suras (Alif Lām Mīm, Yā Sīn, etc.). Niasse discusses multiple interpretive traditions — grammatical, mystical, and prophetic — and gives his own reading, which tends toward the Sufi understanding of these letters as keys to divine secrets.

Direct, experiential knowledge of God — the supreme object of the spiritual path in Niasse's Tijānī tradition. Mishri distinguishes maʿrifa from mere doctrinal knowledge: the Qurʾān possesses an outward dimension (the foundation of the sharīʿa, governing right action) and an inward dimension (the foundation of the ḥaqīqa, which is the domain of maʿrifa). While the sharīʿa is sealed and admits no addition, the inward undergoes 'constant renewal in regards to how its meanings become manifest' for the ʿārifūn — those who have attained maʿrifa. In Niasse's own usage, maʿrifa is 'the spirit of the intellect, and the intellect is the spirit of the body' (Lesson 16, Q. 6:36–110), situating it at the apex of the spiritual faculties hierarchy.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: المعرفة روح روح الروح، المعرفة روح العقل، والعقل روح الجسد
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Maʿrifa is the spirit of the spirit of the spirit; maʿrifa is the spirit of the intellect, and the intellect is the spirit of the body

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: والجنة المعنوية هي المعرفة بالله تبارك وتعالى
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The inward Garden is maʿrifa of God

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: الرؤية التي هي معرفة الله، معرفة اللّٰه أولا بالإيمان
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The vision that is maʿrifa of God — maʿrifa of God first through faith

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: ولاية الكبرى من عرف اللّٰه معرفة فوق الإيمان وفوق الإيقان بل معرفة المشاهدة والعيان
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler citing Niasse's distinction between levels of maʿrifa

mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018), Q. 17 (pp. 36–37).
mishri_quote: Allah's speech... has an outward and an inward: its outward is the foundation of the sharīʿa, and its inward is the foundation of the haqīqah... constant renewal happens in regards to how its meanings become manifest.

definition: [PENDING — awaiting AK's definition drawing on Mishri]

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: مشرب السر الأنس والهيبة
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The drinking-place of the sirr is intimacy (uns) and awe (hayba)

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: والسر يفنى في ذات اللّٰه تبارك وتعالى
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The sirr becomes annihilated in the Essence of God

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: ولابد من الذكر باللسان أولا ثم بالقلب ثم بالروح والسر حتى يصل إلى المطلوب
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Dhikr must proceed: tongue, then heart (qalb), then spirit (rūḥ), then sirr — until the sought arrives

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: لباس النفس ولباس القلب ولباس الروح ولباس السر
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler on the four vestments of spiritual faculties: nafs, qalb, rūḥ, sirr

definition: [PENDING — awaiting AK's definition drawing on Mishri]

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: المعرفة روح العقل، والعقل روح الجسد
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Maʿrifa is the spirit of the intellect; the intellect is the spirit of the body

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: الروح هو السلطان والعقل هو الوزير والقلب هو المفتي
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The rūḥ is the sovereign, the intellect is the vizier, the heart is the jurist

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: مشرب الروح مشاهدة الجمال والجلال
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The drinking-place of the rūḥ is the vision of Beauty (jamāl) and Majesty (jalāl)

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: ولباس الروح التقوى ولكن تقوى يمنعه من الالتفات إلى غير اللّٰه
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler on the vestment of the rūḥ: taqwā that prevents turning toward other than God

definition: The Muhammadan Reality — the first of the divine self-disclosures (tajalliyyāt) and the metaphysical principle through which creation proceeds. Mishri provides a full cosmological account: 'To bring these ḥaqāʾiq and nisbahs into existence, Allah brought the Ḥaqīqa Muḥammadiyya into existence, for were it not for that, Allah's relation to creation would not have appeared or been known.' It is the 'shadow' of the Aḥadiyya, the locus of the divine Names and their sealhood (khatmiyya), and the barzakh between the pure divine and the created order. In Niasse's tafsīr, it is the first tajallī: 'When God willed to manifest an existence other than His own, He disclosed Himself in the Ḥaqīqa Muḥammadiyya — it is the first tajallī after sheer hiddenness' (Lesson 13, Q. 4:148–5:22).

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: لما أراد إظهار وجود غير وجوده تجلى في الحقيقة المحمدية فهي أول تجلٍ بعد الغيب المحض
niasse_evidence.translation_note: When God willed to manifest an existence other than His own, He disclosed Himself in the Muhammadan Reality — it is the first tajallī after sheer hiddenness

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: هذا الروح هو الحقيقة المحمدية
niasse_evidence.translation_note: This spirit is the Muhammadan Reality

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: اللّٰه تبارك وتعالى أول ما أوجد درة بيضاء وهي الحقيقة المحمدية
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The first thing God brought into existence was a white pearl — that is the Muhammadan Reality

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: هذا الروح هو الذي به ضل النصارى
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler contextualising the Muhammadan Reality against Christian deviation

mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018), Q. 15.
mishri_quote: To bring these ḥaqāʾiq into existence, Allah brought the Ḥaqīqa Muḥammadiyya into existence, for were it not for that, Allah's relation to creation would not have appeared or been known.

definition: The Divine Presence — in Mishri's account, the term denotes the metaphysical stations or modes through which the divine self-disclosure (tajallī) operates. The ḥaḍarāt are the loci through which the Qurʾān 'descended' and through which the divine Names and Attributes are manifest. Mishri connects them directly to the four ḥaqāʾiq: the ḥaqīqa Muḥammadiyya, the realm of the Names, the realm of the spirits, and the realm of sensible existence. In Niasse's usage, the ḥaḍara is the spiritual goal of the murīd: 'until you find intimacy and awe in the pavilions of the Divine Presence' (Lesson 23, Q. 9:100–129).

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: قصدوا الحضرة، إذا صدقوا في أنّهم لا يقصدون غير الحضرة فهم لا مقصود عندهم إلا الله
niasse_evidence.translation_note: They aimed for the Divine Presence (ḥaḍra); if they were sincere in seeking nothing but the ḥaḍra, then they have no goal but God

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: إلى أن تجد الأنس والهيبة بسرادقات الحضرة
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Until you find intimacy and awe in the pavilions of the Divine Presence

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: إذا ضربنا هذه الأسماء في الحضرات الثلاث الحقائق الثلاث
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler on the three ḥaḍarāt — the three divine presences

mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018), Q. 15.
mishri_quote: The Exalted Essence was and there was nothing other than it... It wanted to be known, so it manifested in itself, for itself, through itself.

definition: [PENDING — awaiting AK's definition drawing on Mishri]

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: بدعوى الوصول إلى اللّٰه تبارك وتعالى بدون السلوك
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Claiming to reach God without sulūk [Niasse dismisses such claims as ẓulm]

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: سلوك غير الأنبياء من النفس الأمارة بالسوء إلى أن تصل إلى الكمال، وسلوك الأنبياء من النفس المطمئنة
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The sulūk of non-prophets proceeds from the commanding soul (nafs ammāra) toward perfection; the sulūk of prophets begins from the tranquil soul (nafs muṭmaʾinna)

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: الإخوان في السلوك المريدون الصادقون ينتفعون بعضهم من بعض
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler: brothers in sulūk — sincere murīdūn benefit one another

definition: [PENDING — awaiting AK's definition drawing on Mishri]

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: فاضت عليها فيضة أخرى من رحمة اللّٰه تبارك وتعالى
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Another outpouring (fayḍa) of divine mercy flooded over it [the soul]

apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler citing Ibn ʿArabī on fayḍ via Fayḍ al-Qadīr

definition: Vigilant-awareness — the sustained consciousness of being seen by God, which Mishri identifies as the primary differentiator of spiritual rank. Murāqaba is the station below mushāhada (direct vision), and Niasse positions it as the minimum attainment of the Sufi path: 'If you cannot reach the station of mushāhada, at least reach the station of murāqaba' (Lesson 30, Q. 16:90–17:111). Mishri elaborates: 'People differ in their standing with Allah according to the degree of their murāqaba, and the people of hearts are the people of Allah.' The station is practised through attending to God's presence at all times, and is the foundation of all subsequent spiritual realisation.
mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Words of Mishri (unpublished transcriptions, n.d.), authorised by Shaykh Ibrāhīm Niasse
mishri_quote: People differ in their standing with Allah according to the degree of their murāqabah, and the people of hearts are the people of Allah. Whoever wishes to be foremost and not overtaken must hasten toward murāqabah.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: إن لم تقدر أن تصل إلى مرتبة المشاهدة فلا أقل من أن تصل إلى مرتبة المراقبة
niasse_evidence.translation_note: If you cannot reach the station of mushāhada (direct vision), at least reach the station of murāqaba

definition: Spiritual aspiration — the directed force of the soul toward God and toward God's people. In Niasse's tafsīr sessions, the opening poem of every lesson is an invocation of the Shaykh's himma: 'Yā himmat al-shaykh uḥḍurī / lanā bi-hādhā al-maḥḍar / wa-ltaṭifī bi-naẓra / tātī lanā bi-l-ẓafar' (O aspiration of the Shaykh, be present with us in this gathering; look upon us with a glance that brings us victory). The invocation across all thirty lessons places himma at the threshold of Qurʾānic engagement — it is the spiritual condition of possibility for exegesis. Mishri distinguishes himma from related terms: 'Aspiration (himma) is of three types: zeal (himma), care (ihtimām), and worry (hamma). Whatever is for Allah and in Allah is zeal (himma).' This positions himma as the highest of the three — desire directed entirely toward the divine.
mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Words of Mishri (unpublished transcriptions, n.d.), authorised by Shaykh Ibrāhīm Niasse
mishri_quote: Aspiration is of three types: zeal (himma), care (ihtimām), and worry (hamma). Whatever is for Allah and in Allah is zeal.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: يا همة الشيخ احضري لنا بهذا المحضر ولتعطفي بنظرة تأتي لنا بالظفر
niasse_evidence.translation_note: O aspiration of the Shaykh, be present with us in this gathering; look upon us with a glance that brings us victory [opening invocation of all 30 lessons]

definition: Repentance and return — the act of turning back to God from heedlessness or transgression. In the Tijānī tradition, tawba is not merely moral amendment but a continuous spiritual orientation: Mishri states that 'any time that passes without murāqabah — let one be certain that what was lost of goodness in that moment exceeds all that was gained throughout one's entire past. Let him return to Allah and reclaim what has passed by saying, Innā lillāh wa-innā ilayhi rājiʿūn.' This frames tawba not as a single act of contrition but as a sustained return. In Niasse's tafsīr, tawba appears across multiple lessons as a condition for spiritual advancement, most explicitly in Lesson 10: 'He had spent his life in error; today I have cut my zunnār and here I am returning to You in tawba.' The formula of return — tawba followed by renewed orientation toward God — is the structuring logic of the Sufi path.
mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Words of Mishri (unpublished transcriptions, n.d.), authorised by Shaykh Ibrāhīm Niasse
mishri_quote: Any time that passes without murāqabah — let one be certain that what was lost of goodness in that moment exceeds all that was gained throughout one's entire past. Let him return to Allah and reclaim what has passed by saying: Innā lillāh wa-innā ilayhi rājiʿūn.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: قضى عمره في الضلالة، واليوم قطعت زناري وها أنا رجعت إليك بالتوبة
niasse_evidence.translation_note: He had spent his life in error; today I have cut my zunnār and here I am returning to You in tawba

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: أراد أن يقبل منا التوبة فنرجع إلى اللّٰه تبارك وتعالى
niasse_evidence.translation_note: He willed to accept our repentance so that we return to God
